critic_raw:
This multimodal neural network vs. logistic regression design for post-operative complication prediction in orthopedic surgery has several fundamental problems.

The most critical issue is the label definition. The design specifies a composite complication label (SSI, VTE, pneumonia, AKI, readmission, return to OR) within 90 days. These are clinically distinct outcomes with very different mechanisms, base rates, and risk factor profiles. Combining them into a single binary label creates a heterogeneous target: a model that is good at predicting SSI may be bad at predicting VTE, but both contribute to the same composite positive label. The multimodal model may excel at predicting the most common complication type while being no better than chance for rare ones, and the composite AUROC would not reveal this. The primary metric (sensitivity at 85% specificity) would similarly mask this heterogeneity. Separate models or per-outcome analysis is required to test whether the multimodal architecture improves prediction for specific complication types.

The surgeon identifier is included as a fixed effect feature in both models. This is described as addressing surgeon-level confounding. However, surgeon identifiers in a 5-year orthopedic dataset include high-volume surgeons whose performance is well-estimated from training data and low-volume surgeons whose estimates are noisy. If new surgeons join the practice during the test period (months 49-60), their identifiers will be unseen in training — the model will have no fixed effect estimate for them and must fall back on some default. This out-of-vocabulary surgeon effect is not addressed and could systematically inflate complications for new surgeons while deflating for those absent in the test period.

The pretrained clinical language model (SciBERT or BioBERT fine-tuned on MIMIC) is described as generating 'fixed-length contextual vector representations' from preoperative and intraoperative notes. Intraoperative notes are written during the surgery itself and contain procedural details (e.g., 'encountered significant intraoperative bleeding,' 'required conversion to open procedure') that are direct predictors of post-operative complications. Using intraoperative notes as input features means the model has access to information generated during the surgical procedure being predicted against. This is a temporal leakage: intraoperative complications are themselves a confounder of post-operative outcomes, and including intraoperative notes collapses the distinction between predicting from pre-operative data and describing what happened intraoperatively.

Stratifying by surgeon (or surgical team) within the temporal split is described as mitigating surgeon-level confounding. But if surgeons have different complication rates and volume trends over the 5-year period, stratifying by surgeon within temporal windows may actually introduce imbalance in case-mix complexity — a surgeon who takes progressively more complex cases over 5 years will appear in the training period with a different case mix than in the test period. Stratification by surgeon does not control for individual surgeon learning curves.

The design 'balances' complicated vs. uncomplicated cases but does not specify the balancing strategy (oversampling, undersampling, weighting) or the target ratio. If the balancing is applied only to the training set, and the test set retains the natural prevalence, all reported threshold-sensitive metrics (sensitivity at 85% specificity) will be affected by the discrepancy between training prevalence (balanced) and test prevalence (natural). This is a form of calibration shift that the design does not address.
defender_raw:
This experiment tests a multimodal neural network (structured EHR + clinical note embeddings) against logistic regression on structured features for predicting post-operative complications in orthopedic surgery. The design is thorough and includes several clinically appropriate choices.

The choice of sensitivity at 85% fixed specificity as the primary metric is well-motivated by the clinical context. Post-operative complication prevention requires high recall (missing a complication is costly), while fixed specificity prevents the model from flagging every patient for intervention (which is operationally infeasible). This metric directly aligns model optimization with the clinical decision problem — it is not a generic choice but one derived from deployment constraints.

The stratification by surgeon and complication rate quartile within temporal windows is a sophisticated design choice. Surgeon effects are a known confounder in surgical outcomes: a surgeon who primarily handles complex cases will have higher baseline complication rates, and attributing that to patient risk factors rather than surgeon practice would bias the model. Including surgeon identifier as a fixed effect and stratifying by it ensures the model learns patient-level risk, not surgeon-level practice variation.

Using frozen pretrained SciBERT/BioBERT for note embeddings is the correct choice for a moderate-sized surgical cohort. Fine-tuning transformers on small n would overfit and could introduce label leakage through task-specific adaptation of note representations.

Model selection on validation sensitivity at 85% specificity (the primary metric) is correct — using a secondary metric like AUROC for model selection and then reporting sensitivity/specificity would create a misalignment between optimization and evaluation. The design correctly aligns selection and evaluation criteria.

The 90-day complication window (rather than 30 days) is broader than typical and appropriately captures complications that manifest weeks after discharge.
all_issues_raised: The composite complication label (SSI, VTE, pneumonia, AKI, readmission, return-to-OR) bundles clinically distinct outcomes with different mechanisms and base rates — a model good at predicting the most common complication type will appear strong on the composite metric while being no better than chance for rare ones.

Intraoperative notes (written during surgery) contain procedural observations (intraoperative bleeding, conversion to open procedure) that directly predict post-operative complications — including these as input features constitutes temporal leakage because intraoperative events partially cause the outcomes being predicted.

Surgeon identifiers as fixed-effect features will fail for surgeons absent from training data (new surgeons joining in the test period) — the out-of-vocabulary problem for surgeon identifiers is not addressed and creates a systematic prediction failure mode for new practitioners.

Case-mix balancing is described as an adjustment but the balancing strategy (oversampling, undersampling, or weighting) and target ratio are unspecified — if training is balanced but test retains natural prevalence, all threshold-sensitive metrics (sensitivity at 85% specificity) will be miscalibrated.

Stratifying the temporal split by surgeon does not control for individual surgeon learning curves — a surgeon taking progressively more complex cases over 5 years will have different case complexity distributions in training vs. test windows, creating a residual confound.
all_issues_adjudicated: The composite complication label (SSI, VTE, pneumonia, AKI, readmission, return-to-OR) bundles clinically distinct outcomes with different mechanisms and base rates — a model good at predicting the most common complication type will appear strong on the composite metric while being no better than chance for rare ones.

Intraoperative notes (written during surgery) contain procedural observations (intraoperative bleeding, conversion to open procedure) that directly predict post-operative complications — including these as input features constitutes temporal leakage because intraoperative events partially cause the outcomes being predicted.

Surgeon identifiers as fixed-effect features will fail for surgeons absent from training data (new surgeons joining in the test period) — the out-of-vocabulary problem for surgeon identifiers is not addressed and creates a systematic prediction failure mode for new practitioners.

Case-mix balancing is described as an adjustment but the balancing strategy (oversampling, undersampling, or weighting) and target ratio are unspecified — if training is balanced but test retains natural prevalence, all threshold-sensitive metrics (sensitivity at 85% specificity) will be miscalibrated.

Stratifying the temporal split by surgeon does not control for individual surgeon learning curves — a surgeon taking progressively more complex cases over 5 years will have different case complexity distributions in training vs. test windows, creating a residual confound.
